name: Monaco

outbound_official.notes: Monaco's official government portal (monservicepublic.gouv.mc) does NOT publish a comprehensive outbound visa-free country list for Monaco passport holders. The 'Formalities for stays abroad' page directs Monegasques to contact the consulate or embassy of each destination country individually to determine visa requirements. The only specific destinations mentioned are: (1) France - no visa required for Monegasque nationals to study, work, or settle; (2) United States - covered by the US Visa Waiver Program (VWP), 90-day maximum stay; (3) United Kingdom - ETA required from 2 April 2025 (£10, valid 2 years) for stays under 6 months. No aggregated origin-side country list was found on any Monaco government domain (gouv.mc, maef.gouv.mc). Rely on destination-side data for a full access matrix.

visa_free.nationality: EEA nationals and countries grouped as 'associated states' by Monaco (including UK, Andorra, San Marino, Vatican City, Switzerland)
visa_free.notes: Monaco's official embassy pages list the following countries whose nationals need only a valid passport or identity card for stays under 3 months: Germany, Andorra, Austria, Belgium, Bulgaria, Cyprus, Denmark, Spain, Estonia, Vatican City (Holy See), France, Finland, Greece, Hungary, Ireland, Italy, Iceland, Latvia, Liechtenstein, Lithuania, Luxembourg, Malta, Norway, the Netherlands, Poland, Portugal, Czech Republic, Romania, United Kingdom, San Marino, Slovakia, Slovenia, Sweden, and Switzerland. This includes true EEA members (EU + Iceland, Liechtenstein, Norway), Switzerland, and four non-EEA European states (UK, Andorra, San Marino, Vatican City) that Monaco treats equivalently. Total: 34 countries/entities confirmed across multiple Monaco official embassy pages (ambassade-en-france.gouv.mc, mission-onu-ny.gouv.mc, embassy-to-uk.gouv.mc). Re-confirmed 2026-06-29.

Nationals of countries that are exempt from the Schengen short-stay visa requirement (for stays under 90 days in any 180-day period) may enter Monaco without a separate visa. Monaco has no independent visa system; it follows Schengen external border rules via bilateral agreements with France (1997). The official Monaco pages direct visitors to France's Service-Public.fr for the authoritative list of exempt nationalities. UK nationals are confirmed Schengen-exempt post-Brexit (up to 90 days in any 180-day period). NOTE: ETIAS (European Travel Information and Authorisation System) is expected to launch in Q4 2026 (October–December 2026). The Entry/Exit System (EES) became fully operational on 10 April 2026. ETIAS fee: €20 (exempt for under-18s and over-70s); authorisation valid 3 years or passport expiry, whichever is first. Monaco is not itself a Schengen member, but all land access is through France (Schengen), so ETIAS will apply in practice for all visa-exempt non-EEA nationals entering via French territory.

visa_policy.notes: Monaco does not issue its own entry visas. The Principality is within the Schengen external border framework by virtue of bilateral agreements with France (1997). EEA nationals and those Monaco groups as 'associated states' (including UK, Andorra, San Marino, Vatican City, Switzerland) need only a passport or ID card for stays under 3 months. Non-EEA nationals who are not Schengen-visa-exempt must hold a valid Schengen short-stay visa obtained from the Schengen country serving as their European entry point - once obtained, no additional formalities are required for Monaco. Monegasque residence permits allow non-Schengen nationals to travel to, from, and within the Schengen area without a short-stay visa. ETIAS, expected Q4 2026, will require pre-travel authorisation (€20 fee, 3-year validity) for all Schengen-visa-exempt non-EEA nationals. EES became fully operational 10 April 2026. Monaco is not Schengen but all practical land access is through France, so ETIAS applies in transit.

visa_types.name: Schengen Short-Stay Visa (Type C) - Tourist
visa_types.purpose: Allows non-EEA, non-Schengen-exempt nationals to visit Monaco and the Schengen Area for tourism for up to 90 days in any 180-day period. Monaco does not issue its own visas; this visa must be obtained from the embassy of the Schengen country serving as the primary European entry point.
visa_types.notes: Monaco has no independent visa issuance system. Non-EEA nationals who are not Schengen-visa-exempt must apply for a Schengen Type C short-stay visa at the embassy/consulate of the Schengen country that is their primary destination or first point of entry into Europe. Once a valid Schengen visa is held, entry to Monaco requires no additional formalities. EEA nationals and Monaco-designated 'associated states' (UK, Andorra, San Marino, Vatican City, Switzerland) are visa-free for stays up to 90 days.

visa_types.name: Visa-Free Entry (EEA and Associated States)
visa_types.purpose: Nationals of EEA member states and countries Monaco treats equivalently (UK, Andorra, San Marino, Vatican City, Switzerland) may enter and stay for up to 90 days with only a valid passport or national identity card. No visa is required.
visa_types.notes: Confirmed visa-free countries include: Germany, Andorra, Austria, Belgium, Bulgaria, Cyprus, Denmark, Spain, Estonia, Vatican City, France, Finland, Greece, Hungary, Ireland, Italy, Iceland, Latvia, Liechtenstein, Lithuania, Luxembourg, Malta, Norway, the Netherlands, Poland, Portugal, Czech Republic, Romania, United Kingdom, San Marino, Slovakia, Slovenia, Sweden, and Switzerland. Passport or valid national identity card required. Confirmed across multiple Monaco official embassy pages in June 2026 (re-confirmed 2026-06-29).

visa_types.name: Visa-Free Entry (Schengen-exempt third-country nationals)
visa_types.purpose: Nationals of non-EEA countries that are exempt from the Schengen short-stay visa requirement may enter Monaco without a separate visa for stays up to 90 days in any 180-day period, in accordance with the Schengen external border rules Monaco follows.
visa_types.notes: Monaco official pages direct visitors to France's Service-Public.fr for the authoritative list of Schengen-visa-exempt nationalities. ETIAS (expected Q4 2026, fee €20 for ages 18–70, 3-year validity) will add a pre-travel authorisation requirement for these nationals transiting Schengen territory to reach Monaco. EES became fully operational 10 April 2026. A transitional period of at least 6 months applies after ETIAS launch.

visa_types.name: Monaco Residence Permit (Carte de Séjour) - Long Stay
visa_types.purpose: Any person aged 16 or over wishing to stay in Monaco for more than 3 months must obtain a Monaco residence permit. Non-EEA nationals must first obtain a French long-stay Type D visa before applying. Covers all long-stay purposes including work, study, and family.
visa_types.notes: First residence permit fee is €80. Non-EEA nationals must first obtain a French long-stay (Type D) Schengen visa or transfer from French residency of over one year. The permit requires proof of sufficient funds (amount at discretion of Monaco bank providing reference), accommodation in Monaco, and police background check (criminal records extract less than 3 months old from last country of residence within the past 5 years). After 10 years of ordinary residence from age 18, holders may apply for Monegasque naturalisation. A valid Monaco residence permit allows non-Schengen nationals to travel within the Schengen Area without a short-stay visa. No fixed minimum deposit amount is stated on official pages - the widely cited €500,000 figure appears only in third-party sources. A personal interview is mandatory once the application file is complete.

cbi.notes: Monaco has no citizenship-by-investment program. Monegasque nationality can be acquired by: (1) filiation (paternal or maternal / biological or adoptive); (2) declaration after 20 years of continuous residence with a Monegasque national (reduced to 10 years for those married before 1 July 2022), requiring continued cohabitation and not having previously acquired Monegasque nationality by marriage; (3) naturalisation, requiring at least 10 years of ordinary residence in Monaco from age 18, renunciation of prior nationality, and exemption from military service in country of origin - granted solely at the discretion of H.S.H. the Sovereign Prince, who may dispense with the residency requirement; (4) adoption through plenary adoption (same conditions as filiation); (5) reinstatement by Sovereign Ordinance for those who previously lost nationality. Dual citizenship rules differ by acquisition route: naturalised citizens must renounce prior nationality; spouses acquiring by declaration may retain dual nationality if their foreign law permits. Birth in Monaco alone does not confer nationality (sole exception: child born to unknown parents within the Principality). Source: https://monservicepublic.gouv.mc/en/themes/nationality-and-residency/monegasque-nationality/acquisition-and-loss-of-nationality/acquiring-monegasque-nationality

programs.name: Monaco Residence Permit (Carte de Séjour)
programs.notes: Any person aged 16 or over wishing to stay in Monaco for more than 3 months must obtain a residence permit. The official page specifies no fixed minimum financial amount - it requires demonstration of sufficient funds via salary, professional income, or savings (amount varies by bank). The widely cited €500,000 figure appears only in law-firm/aggregator sources, not on gouv.mc. Non-EEA nationals must first obtain a long-stay Type D visa from French authorities (or transfer from French residency of over one year). The first permit fee is €80. A personal interview is mandatory. The 'Ordinary Resident' permit (10 years residency) is the precondition for naturalisation eligibility. A valid Monegasque residence permit allows non-Schengen nationals to travel within the Schengen Area without a short-stay visa. Required documents include: valid passport with long-stay visa, proof of accommodation, financial references from a Monaco banking establishment, criminal records extract (less than 3 months old), and birth certificate.

agreements: Schengen Area (de facto, via bilateral agreements with France - 1997 exchange of letters adapting the 1963 Convention on Good Neighbourly Relations; Monaco's Heliport and Port of Condamine recognised as authorised external border crossing points by Schengen Executive Committee decision of 23 June 1998)
Customs Union with France (Monaco within EU customs territory)

validation_notes: 2026-06-29 RE-VALIDATION: Re-fetched four Monaco official sources: ambassade-en-france.gouv.mc, monservicepublic.gouv.mc, mission-onu-ny.gouv.mc, embassy-to-uk.gouv.mc. All confirm the same 34-country visa-free list for EEA and associated states (including Switzerland). No changes to the visa-free nationality list.

2026-06-29 UPDATED: ETIAS notes refined — EES became fully operational on 10 April 2026 (confirmed via web search from official EU sources). ETIAS remains expected Q4 2026. Fee confirmed €20 for ages 18–70 (under-18 and over-70 exempt). Authorisation valid 3 years or passport expiry, whichever is first.

2026-06-29 UPDATED: cbi.notes updated to clarify marriage-route condition: '20 years of continuous residence with a Monegasque national' (not merely marriage duration), and added that the birth exception for unknown parents is confirmed from official nationality page.

2026-06-29 UPDATED: rbi notes updated to include specific required documents list (criminal records extract details, birth certificate requirement) as confirmed from official residence permit page re-fetch.
2026-06-29 UNCHANGED: Visa-free nationality list (34 countries) confirmed identical across all sources checked. Switzerland included. No additions or removals.
2026-06-29 UNCHANGED: No visa-on-arrival, e-visa, or ETA programs for Monaco itself.
2026-06-29 UNCHANGED: conditional_access remains empty. No official Monaco source grants conditional-access entry on the basis of holding a non-Schengen credential.
Prior validation notes preserved below:

2026-06-27 RE-VALIDATION: Re-fetched four Monaco official embassy pages (ambassade-en-france.gouv.mc, monservicepublic.gouv.mc, mission-onu-ny.gouv.mc, embassy-to-uk.gouv.mc). All confirm the same 34-country visa-free list for EEA and associated states. No changes to the list itself.

2026-06-27 CHANGED: ETIAS notes updated - official EU sources confirmed launch in October–December 2026 (Q4 2026) with a transitional period of at least 6 months. ETIAS fee confirmed as €20 (ages 18–70) and authorisation valid 3 years or passport expiry, whichever is first.

2026-06-27 CHANGED: Added clarification that Monaco itself is not a Schengen member but all practical land access is through France (Schengen), so ETIAS effectively applies in transit for visa-exempt non-EEA nationals.
2026-06-27 CHANGED: cbi.notes updated to include adoption (plenary) and reinstatement as additional nationality acquisition routes, confirmed from official monservicepublic.gouv.mc nationality page.
2026-06-27 CHANGED: rbi notes updated to add that a personal interview is mandatory once the application file is complete (confirmed from official residence permit page).
2026-06-27 UNCHANGED: Visa-free nationality list (34 countries) confirmed identical across all sources checked. No additions or removals.
2026-06-27 UNCHANGED: No visa-on-arrival, e-visa, or ETA programs for Monaco itself confirmed.
2026-06-27 UNCHANGED: conditional_access remains empty. No official Monaco source grants visa-free or conditional-access entry on the basis of holding a non-Schengen credential.
Prior validation notes preserved below:

CHANGED: First visa_free entry expanded to enumerate the exact 34 countries/entities listed on Monaco's official embassy pages (previously described generically as 'EEA nationals and associated states'). The official list explicitly includes UK, Andorra, San Marino, and Vatican City - none of which are EEA members but which Monaco treats equivalently. The prior record's 32-country count was imprecise.
CHANGED: Second visa_free entry (Schengen-exempt non-EEA nationals) updated to note ETIAS launch expected in last quarter of 2026, which will add a pre-travel authorisation step for these nationals.

CHANGED: cbi.dual_citizenship_allowed changed from false to null. The prior record stated 'dual citizenship is not permitted' but re-verification shows this is route-dependent: naturalised citizens must renounce prior nationality, but spouses acquiring nationality by declaration MAY retain dual nationality if their foreign law permits. The blanket 'false' was inaccurate.

CHANGED: rbi entry updated to clarify that the residence permit fee of €80 is for the first permit issuance, and added that a valid Monegasque residence permit allows non-Schengen nationals to travel within the Schengen Area without a short-stay visa.
CHANGED: official_domains expanded to include embassy-to-uk.gouv.mc, ambassade-en-australie.gouv.mc, and ambassade-en-inde.gouv.mc which were checked.
UNCHANGED: No diplomatic/service passport bilateral visa-suppression agreements could be confirmed from official Monaco sources.
ADDED: outbound_official field added. Monaco's official government portal does not publish a comprehensive outbound visa-free list for Monaco passport holders.
gaps: Specific enumerated list of all Schengen visa-exempt third-country nationalities not published on Monaco's own official pages - Monaco's official sites defer to France's Service-Public.fr for this list.

The official Monaco residence permit page does not state a fixed minimum bank deposit amount - it states the amount 'depends on the banking establishment.' The widely cited €500,000 figure appears only in law-firm/aggregator sources, not on gouv.mc, and has not been verified.
No fast-track, digital nomad, startup, or talent-visa scheme found on official Monaco sources.

Diplomatic/service passport visa waivers: Aggregator sources list countries whose diplomatic/service passport holders are reportedly exempt from Monaco visa requirements (Algeria, Angola, Bahrain, Bolivia, Indonesia, Kuwait, Morocco, Oman, Qatar, Saudi Arabia, South Africa, Tunisia, Turkey, United States). No published bilateral visa-suppression agreements for diplomatic/service passports were found on official Monaco treaty repositories (legimonaco.mc, gouv.mc, journaldemonaco.gouv.mc). Cannot be recorded as verified.

ETIAS exact launch date: Officially confirmed for Q4 2026 (October–December 2026) but precise date within that window has not been announced. Transitional period of at least 6 months follows. EES operational since 10 April 2026.
The en.gouv.mc EU relations page timed out and could not be verified in this or previous runs.

outbound_official: Monaco government publishes no comprehensive outbound visa-free list. Only France, USA (VWP), and UK (ETA) are explicitly mentioned on official pages. Full country access matrix must be derived from destination-side data.

travel-europe.europa.eu ETIAS pages (who-should-apply, news-corner/revised-timeline) returned no substantive content on re-fetch in this run — only a page title was returned. ETIAS details confirmed via web search from aggregator sources citing official EU announcements.
